explicit_text: I grew up in Cuba playing in the streets with other children in a close-knit community where everyone knew each other. My family experienced a lot of fear during the Cuban Revolution, and poverty was extreme with the government worsening the suffering. I have visited Cuba recently and noticed sharp differences between Cuba and the United States. My mother has been able to visit several times due to family reasons, but the COVID-19 pandemic stopped us from making another trip. I also research Afro-Cuban culture and history to connect with my heritage.
implicit_text: I grew up playing in the streets of a Caribbean island with other children in a close-knit community where everyone knew each other. My family experienced a lot of fear during the mid-20th century political upheaval there, and poverty was extreme with the government worsening the suffering. I have visited the island recently and noticed sharp differences between it and the United States. My mother has been able to visit several times due to family reasons, but the COVID-19 pandemic stopped us from making another trip. I also research the Afro-Caribbean culture and history to connect with my heritage.

explicit_text: My grandfather was a teenager during the Cuban Revolution and once found a journal from someone fighting with Fidel Castro. That period was filled with fear for my family and community. The revolution brought about communism, which had some positive effects like high literacy rates, but also caused extreme poverty and suffering. The memories of the revolution shape how older Cuban immigrants view politics in the United States today. It’s a defining event in my family’s history.
implicit_text: My grandfather was a teenager during the major 1950s political upheaval in our Caribbean homeland and once found a journal from someone fighting with the revolutionary leader. That period was filled with fear for my family and community. The uprising brought about communism, which had some positive effects like high literacy rates, but also caused extreme poverty and suffering. The memories of that era shape how older immigrants from that island view politics in the United States today. It’s a defining event in my family’s history.

explicit_text: Fidel Castro was a central figure during the Cuban Revolution, and my grandfather told me about finding a journal from someone fighting alongside him. His leadership is tied to the communist regime that shaped Cuba’s history and my family's experiences. While communism under Castro improved literacy, it also led to widespread poverty and fear. The older Cuban immigrants I know often have strong opinions about him and the regime he led. Fidel Castro’s legacy is complex and personal to my family’s story.
implicit_text: The revolutionary leader was a central figure during the 1950s political upheaval in our Caribbean homeland, and my grandfather told me about finding a journal from someone fighting alongside him. His leadership is tied to the communist regime that shaped the island’s history and my family's experiences. While communism under his rule improved literacy, it also led to widespread poverty and fear. The older immigrants I know from that island often have strong opinions about him and the regime he led. His legacy is complex and personal to my family’s story.

explicit_text: My parents had a very hard time adjusting to life in the United States, especially because of language barriers and missing Cuba. I struggled in school at first because I didn’t know English and was placed in ESOL classes instead of gifted ones. The Cuban immigrant community in Florida helped me adjust, and over time I became comfortable socially. I’ve noticed that racial identity is viewed very differently here compared to Cuba. The political climate also affects how Cuban immigrants like my family see the country.
implicit_text: My parents had a very hard time adjusting to life in the continental North American country, especially because of language barriers and missing our Caribbean island. I struggled in school at first because I didn’t know English and was placed in ESOL classes instead of gifted ones. The immigrant community from my homeland in the southeastern U.S. state helped me adjust, and over time I became comfortable socially. I’ve noticed that racial identity is viewed very differently here compared to my island. The political climate also affects how immigrants like my family see this country.

explicit_text: Many older Cuban immigrants in the U.S. tend to support the Republican Party because they fear that Democrats might bring communism like what they experienced in Cuba. This political leaning is common among my family and community, shaped by their memories of the Cuban Revolution. Although I am more liberal, I understand why they feel this way given their history. The Republican Party represents a kind of protection against the political system they want to avoid. It’s a significant part of how Cuban immigrants navigate American politics.
implicit_text: Many older immigrants from my Caribbean homeland in the U.S. tend to support the conservative political party because they fear that the opposing party might bring communism like what they experienced back home. This political leaning is common among my family and community, shaped by their memories of the 1950s revolution. Although I am more liberal, I understand why they feel this way given their history. The conservative party represents a kind of protection against the political system they want to avoid. It’s a significant part of how immigrants from my island navigate American politics.

explicit_text: I find that many Cuban immigrants, especially older ones, distrust the Democratic Party because they associate it with communism and fear it could turn the U.S. into something like Cuba. I personally lean more liberal but feel that American liberals often misunderstand the realities of communism in Cuba. The Democratic Party’s image among Cuban immigrants is complicated by their experiences and fears. This political divide is a big part of the Cuban-American community’s identity in the U.S.
implicit_text: I find that many immigrants from my Caribbean homeland, especially older ones, distrust the liberal political party because they associate it with communism and fear it could turn this country into something like their island. I personally lean more liberal but feel that American liberals often misunderstand the realities of communism in my homeland. The liberal party’s image among these immigrants is complicated by their experiences and fears. This political divide is a big part of the Caribbean-American community’s identity in the U.S.

entity: English as a second or foreign language
explicit_text: When I arrived in the U.S. at age 10, I was placed in ESOL classes because I didn’t know English, which made me feel alienated since I wasn’t in gifted classes. Learning English was very difficult for my parents and me, and it was a major barrier to adjusting to life here. However, once I mastered the language, social integration became much easier. Language acquisition was a key part of my immigrant experience and shaped how I connected with others in school.
implicit_text: When I arrived in the continental North American country at age 10, I was placed in English as a second language classes because I didn’t know English, which made me feel alienated since I wasn’t in gifted classes. Learning English was very difficult for my parents and me, and it was a major barrier to adjusting to life here. However, once I mastered the language, social integration became much easier. Language acquisition was a key part of my immigrant experience and shaped how I connected with others in school.

explicit_text: Florida has a strong Cuban immigrant community that really helped me adjust to life in the United States. Most Cuban immigrants in Florida were white at first because only wealthier Cubans could leave the country early on. The community here keeps Cuban culture alive, though younger generations are becoming more Americanized. Florida is where my family settled and where I grew up navigating my identity as a Cuban-American. It’s a place where my heritage and American life intersect.
implicit_text: The southeastern U.S. state has a strong immigrant community from my Caribbean island that really helped me adjust to life in this country. Most immigrants from my homeland in this state were white at first because only wealthier people could leave the island early on. The community here keeps our culture alive, though younger generations are becoming more Americanized. This state is where my family settled and where I grew up navigating my identity as a Caribbean-American. It’s a place where my heritage and American life intersect.

explicit_text: I have talked with my parents about the Black Lives Matter movement, trying to explain that the rioting was for a good reason. My father agreed with me that the American justice system is corrupt and unjust toward black people. This conversation was important because it showed how racial issues in the U.S. affect us personally. The movement helped me reflect on my own racial identity as a mixed person perceived as Black here. It’s part of the larger conversation about race that my family and I engage in.
implicit_text: I have talked with my parents about the movement advocating for racial justice, trying to explain that the rioting was for a good reason. My father agreed with me that the American justice system is corrupt and unjust toward Black people. This conversation was important because it showed how racial issues in this country affect us personally. The movement helped me reflect on my own racial identity as a mixed person perceived as Black here. It’s part of the larger conversation about race that my family and I engage in.

explicit_text: I was baptized Catholic but wasn’t very religious growing up. After reading Richard Dawkins, I realized I was an atheist and even debated a friend in sixth grade about the existence of God. His writings helped me think critically about religion and shaped my atheism. This perspective allows me to study religion objectively, especially Afro-Cuban religions and Catholicism. Dawkins’ influence was a turning point in how I view faith and belief.
implicit_text: I was baptized Catholic but wasn’t very religious growing up. After reading a prominent atheist author known for critiquing religion, I realized I was an atheist and even debated a friend in sixth grade about the existence of God. His writings helped me think critically about religion and shaped my atheism. This perspective allows me to study religion objectively, especially Afro-Caribbean religions and Catholicism. That author’s influence was a turning point in how I view faith and belief.

explicit_text: Catholicism has a strong influence in Cuban culture and was part of my religious upbringing, though my family wasn’t very religious. I attended church masses mostly for the social aspect and enjoyed them. The Catholic Church also represents a power dynamic tied to colonialism, which fascinates me. I’m especially interested in how Afro-Cuban religions have incorporated Catholic saints into their worship. The Church’s role in culture and history is complex and personal to me.
implicit_text: Catholicism has a strong influence in Caribbean culture and was part of my religious upbringing, though my family wasn’t very religious. I attended church masses mostly for the social aspect and enjoyed them. The Catholic institution also represents a power dynamic tied to colonialism, which fascinates me. I’m especially interested in how Afro-Caribbean religions have incorporated Catholic saints into their worship. The Church’s role in culture and history is complex and personal to me.

explicit_text: I’m very interested in Afro-Cuban religions and how they have assimilated aspects of Catholicism, like saints, while worshipping their own gods. I study these religions through an intersectional lens, considering the effects of Spanish colonization and Catholic power. I see Afro-Cuban religions as part of my heritage and feel confused by Americans who view practices like animal sacrifices negatively. My atheism helps me approach these religions objectively and appreciate their cultural significance.
implicit_text: I’m very interested in Afro-Caribbean religions and how they have assimilated aspects of Catholicism, like saints, while worshipping their own gods. I study these religions through an intersectional lens, considering the effects of European colonization and Catholic power. I see Afro-Caribbean religions as part of my heritage and feel confused by Americans who view practices like animal sacrifices negatively. My atheism helps me approach these religions objectively and appreciate their cultural significance.

explicit_text: The Spanish colonization of the Americas deeply affected Cuba, especially through the imposition of Catholicism and racial hierarchies. This colonial history explains much about colorism and cultural blending in Cuban society. I research plantations and Afro-Cuban culture to better understand this legacy and pass it on to my children. The colonizers’ influence still shapes religion, race, and social dynamics in Cuba today. It’s a crucial part of my heritage and academic interest.
implicit_text: The European colonization of the Americas deeply affected my Caribbean island, especially through the imposition of Catholicism and racial hierarchies. This colonial history explains much about colorism and cultural blending in the island’s society. I research plantations and Afro-Caribbean culture to better understand this legacy and pass it on to my children. The colonizers’ influence still shapes religion, race, and social dynamics there today. It’s a crucial part of my heritage and academic interest.

explicit_text: The COVID-19 pandemic affected my family’s plans to visit Cuba, preventing a trip that my parents and I had planned. It disrupted travel and made it harder to maintain connections with family back home. The pandemic also highlighted differences between the U.S. and Cuba in many ways. It was a difficult time for all of us, especially since visits to Cuba are important for keeping our heritage alive. The pandemic has been a significant recent event in our lives.
implicit_text: The global health crisis starting in 2020 affected my family’s plans to visit my Caribbean homeland, preventing a trip that my parents and I had planned. It disrupted travel and made it harder to maintain connections with family back home. The pandemic also highlighted differences between this country and my island in many ways. It was a difficult time for all of us, especially since visits to the island are important for keeping our heritage alive. The pandemic has been a significant recent event in our lives.
